figures.caption: Figure 5: Activation Function Ablation. Hard saturation functions (Sign, low-temp Sigmoid) provide superior error masking compared to unbounded ReLU.
Figure 6: Sparsity Ablation. Extreme sparsity (> 90%) acts as a dominant resilience factor, significantly delaying accuracy collapse.

Figure 8: DWN Layer Parity Recovery. for corruption rates p ranging from 0 to 100% and network depths L = 1 to 6. As p → 1, even-depth networks exhibit partial recovery consistent with symmetric error cancellation across layers, whereas odd-depth networks tend toward inverted outputs.
Figure 7: Parameter bit-flip resilience on MNIST, FashionMNIST, KWS and ToyAdmos datasets with corruption rates ranging from 0 to 40%. FP32 and FP16 lines are overlapped with FP8.
Figure 4: Depth Ablation. Deeper networks suffer from error avalanches, confirming the multiplicative error propagation in standard architectures.
Figure 3: Width Ablation. Wider networks (e.g., W = 1024) degrade faster than narrower ones, validating the O(n2) bias accumulation hypothesis.

Figure 1: Illustration of a 2-layer neural network composed entirely of layers of lookup tables with binary values. The outputs of each layer address the LUTs of the next layer, culminating in a popcount for class activations.

Figure 2: Numerical Precision Ablation. Accuracy vs. Bit Error Rate (p) averaged across datasets. A clear hierarchy emerges: floating-point models collapse early (p ≈ 10−5) due to exponent errors, while integer and binary models sustain accuracy significantly longer.
